acct.defender_note: acct=1 (guest) on a token operating against your tenant means a B2B guest is acting. Verify the guest is from an expected partner tenant.

acr.defender_note: acr=0 on a token granting access to sensitive resources is a red flag. acr=1 indicates MFA was performed.
acr.description: Authentication Context Class Reference. Single character: '0' = no MFA-or-equivalent, '1' = MFA satisfied.

acrs.defender_note: Used to enforce authentication context (e.g. require MFA for specific operations).
acrs.description: Authentication Context Class References (multiple). Used in claims-challenge / CAE scenarios.

aio.defender_note: Internal use only. Resources should not validate against it.
aio.description: Internal claim used by Entra for token revalidation. Opaque.

alg.defender_note: RS256 is normal. Anything else is suspicious - either a malformed/forged token or a non-Entra issuer.
alg.description: Signing algorithm. Microsoft Entra always uses RS256 for JWTs.

amr.defender_note: amr=['pwd'] alone with no MFA is a legacy / weak-auth indicator. amr=['mfa','pwd'] is normal. amr=['fed'] with an unexpected federated source is worth investigating.
amr.description: Authentication Method References. Array of methods used to authenticate. pwd=password, mfa=MFA, rsa=RSA token (Authenticator app), pop=proof-of-possession, otp=one-time passcode (email/SMS), fed=federated assertion, wia=Windows Integrated Auth, ngcmfa=advanced credential MFA.

app_displayname.defender_note: Display-only. Real attackers often pick names that mimic legitimate apps - cross-check appid, not the displayname.
app_displayname.description: Display name of the application that requested the token.

appid.defender_note: Cross-reference against the OAuthSentry catalog. If malicious or risky, escalate immediately. Combine with idtyp to know whether it's a delegated or app-only context.
appid.description: Application ID (v1.0 tokens). The OAuth client that initiated the token request.

appidacr.defender_note: appidacr=0 means a public client (typically delegated user flow). appidacr=2 (cert) is best practice for app-only flows; appidacr=1 (secret) is weaker.
appidacr.description: App ID Authentication Class Reference. Indicates how the client authenticated: 0=public client, 1=client secret, 2=client certificate.

aud.defender_note: If aud is unfamiliar, look it up in the known-apps reference. A token with an unexpected audience is being passed somewhere it doesn't belong.
aud.description: Audience. The resource the token is intended for - either an appid (e.g. 00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000 = Microsoft Graph) or a URL (e.g. https://graph.microsoft.com).

auth_time.defender_note: Older auth_time + recent iat = token derived from a refresh-token or session cookie that's been alive a while. Long deltas + sensitive resource access = post-compromise behavior.
auth_time.description: Time at which the user authenticated (Unix epoch).

azp.defender_note: Same as appid. v2.0 tokens use azp; v1.0 tokens use appid.
azp.description: Authorized Party (v2.0 tokens). The OAuth client that initiated the token request - v2.0 equivalent of appid.

azpacr.description: v2.0 equivalent of appidacr. Same values: 0=public, 1=secret, 2=cert.

deviceid.defender_note: Presence indicates the token came from a known device. Recently-registered devices (created in the last 7-30 days) are a UTA0355 / Storm-2372 PRT-phishing signature.
deviceid.description: Device ID from Entra-joined / Entra-registered device.

exp.defender_note: Default access-token lifetime is 60-90 minutes (75 min average). Lifetimes >2 hours indicate a Configurable Token Lifetime (CTL) policy. Lifetimes near 24h (~86400s) are at the CTL maximum and significantly extend post-compromise exposure.

groups.defender_note: Limited to ~150 groups; otherwise an overage claim _claim_names points to Graph for the full list.

iat.defender_note: Combine with exp to compute the token's lifetime window. Large iat-to-event-time deltas indicate refresh-derived tokens.

identityProvider.defender_note: Multiple distinct identityProvider values across users in the same tenant indicates B2B / federation. An unexpected identityProvider on a workforce-user token is worth investigating.
identityProvider.description: The identity provider that authenticated this user (e.g. an external IdP for federated users).

idtyp.defender_note: THE single best one-field tell of token flow type. App-only tokens have empty userId / scopes, populated roles - completely different IR pivots. Required claim for security-aware applications.
idtyp.description: Identity Type. 'user' = delegated token (acting on behalf of a user); 'app' = app-only token (the SP is the principal); 'device' = device token.

ipaddr.defender_note: Cross-check against the user's normal sign-in pattern. Hosting / VPN / Tor IPs on a token claim that issued mailbox-content reads = exfiltration candidate.
ipaddr.description: Source IP address at the time of token issuance.

iss.defender_note: Normal: https://sts.windows.net/<tenant-id>/ (v1) or https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>/v2.0 (v2). External tenants use ciamlogin.com. Anything else is suspicious.
iss.description: Issuer. The Entra STS endpoint that issued the token.

jti.description: JWT ID (RFC 7519). Token identifier; not always present on Entra tokens (Entra prefers uti).

kid.defender_note: Cross-check against login.microsoftonline.com/<tid>/discovery/v2.0/keys if you suspect forgery. Mismatched kid against current Microsoft keys = high-confidence forgery indicator.
kid.description: Key ID. Identifies which Entra signing key was used; must match an entry in the tenant's JWKS.

nbf.defender_note: Almost always equal to iat for Entra tokens.
nbf.description: Not Before time (Unix epoch). Token is invalid before this time.

nonce.defender_note: Most access tokens don't have this. Presence is unusual but not necessarily bad.
nonce.description: Header-level nonce, used in some POP (Proof-of-Possession) and protected token scenarios.

oid.defender_note: The PRIMARY pivot key for IR. Cross-references directly with sign-in logs, M365 audit logs, and the Detection 13 directory lookup.
oid.description: Object ID. The Microsoft Entra object id of the user (delegated tokens) or the service principal (app-only tokens).

platf.defender_note: Cross-check platf against the client's expected runtime. Tokens claiming to be 'Microsoft Office' (FOCI) but issuing from platf=5 (Linux) are suspicious.
platf.description: Platform identifier of the issuing client. 0/1/2/3/4=Windows variants, 5=Linux, 6=iOS, 8=macOS, 14=Android-compat.

policyEvaluated.defender_note: Some Graph calls don't trigger CA evaluation; this is normal but worth knowing during incident reconstruction.
policyEvaluated.description: Whether Conditional Access policy was evaluated for this token.

preferred_username.defender_note: Same as upn - useful for triage, not a security key.
preferred_username.description: v2.0 token equivalent of upn. The user's preferred username.

puid.defender_note: Less commonly used than oid. Stable per-user-per-tenant.

rh.defender_note: Don't rely on this. Internal claim - resources should not use it.
rh.description: Refresh hint. Internal Entra value used to revalidate tokens.

roles.defender_note: On app-only tokens, this is the equivalent of scp - the permissions the SP can exercise. Mail.Read / Mail.ReadWrite / Files.ReadWrite.All on app-only tokens are the post-vendor-compromise smoking guns (Salesloft Drift / Context.ai pattern).
roles.description: Application permissions (app-only tokens) OR app role assignments (delegated tokens). Space-separated.

scp.defender_note: On delegated tokens, scopes define the token's blast radius. Flag *.ReadWrite.All, *.Send, offline_access. Cross-reference against graphpermissions.merill.net for exact endpoint coverage. App-only tokens have empty scp; check roles instead.
scp.description: Scopes (delegated tokens only). Space-separated list of permissions the user consented to for this client. Examples: User.Read, Mail.ReadWrite, Files.ReadWrite.All.

sid.defender_note: Pivot key. Same value as Entra sign-in log SessionId and AppAccessContext.AADSessionId in M365 audit log entries. A session can mint many tokens with different uti values.
sid.description: Session ID. Identifies the Entra sign-in session this token was issued under.

sub.defender_note: Don't use sub for IR pivots; use oid for cross-app identity correlation.
sub.description: Subject. Pairwise per-app identifier - the same user has different sub values per app, so sub alone doesn't tie to a specific human.

tenant_region_scope.defender_note: Useful for cross-checking the user's region against the source IP region.
tenant_region_scope.description: Tenant region scope (e.g. NA, EU, AS, AF, OC, ME).

tid.defender_note: Cross-tenant tokens (where tid is not your tenant) are normal for guest accounts and B2B; verify the tid against your tenant's expected partner list. Unfamiliar tid + appearance in your tenant's logs = pivot to your B2B configuration.
tid.description: Tenant ID where the token was issued.

unique_name.defender_note: Discrepancies between unique_name, upn, and preferred_username are unusual and worth investigating - documented APT29 technique.
unique_name.description: v1.0 token unique-name claim. Often equal to upn.

upn.defender_note: Useful for human-readable triage but should NOT be used as a security key - upn can be changed/spoofed in some scenarios. Use oid instead.
upn.description: User Principal Name. The user's sign-in name (delegated tokens only).

uti.defender_note: THE primary IR pivot. Use to find every Graph call and every audit-log event made with this specific token. See Forensic Traces section 4 for cross-stream pivot SPL.
uti.description: Unique Token Identifier. The opaque per-token identifier; same value as Graph activity log signInActivityId and Entra sign-in log uniqueTokenIdentifier.

ver.defender_note: v1 vs v2 changes which claim names you'll see (appid vs azp, unique_name vs preferred_username). Modern apps use v2.
ver.description: Token version. '1.0' = v1 endpoint, '2.0' = v2 endpoint.

wids.defender_note: The synthetic 'b79fbf4d-...' wid means 'no admin role'. Anything else is a real Entra admin role - look up each GUID against the role table. Privileged roles (Global Administrator, Privileged Role Administrator, etc.) on a stolen token = critical alert.
wids.description: Well-known IDs - tenant role assignments. Array of role template IDs the user holds.

x5t.defender_note: Present on v1.0 tokens, absent on v2.0. Used the same way as kid.
x5t.description: X.509 certificate thumbprint (legacy). Equivalent to kid but uses SHA-1 thumbprint format.

xms_cc.defender_note: CAE-aware tokens get extended lifetime (24-28h vs the default 60-90min) because revocation is real-time. A non-CAE token with 24h lifetime indicates a CTL policy override - audit it.
xms_cc.description: Client Capabilities. Array indicating client capabilities. 'cp1' = client is CAE-aware (Continuous Access Evaluation).

xms_ftd.defender_note: Internal Entra claim. Resources should not validate against xms_* claims.

xms_tcdt.defender_note: Useful for distinguishing brand-new tenants (potentially attacker-created) from established ones.
